exemplar.title: Should Facebook strengthen its Corporate Social Responsibility practices (or improve the process element of the Marketing Mix) in the aftermath of the March 2018 data scandal?

detailedExemplarData.title: Should Facebook strengthen its Corporate Social Responsibility practices (or improve the process element of the Marketing Mix) in the aftermath of the March 2018 data scandal?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Articulates a precise, contextualized topic with clear integrated explanations
Research question is clearly stated, well-focused, and consistently guides the discussion
Methodology employs a diverse range of relevant secondary sources and analytical tools
Introduction provides coherent company background and rationale for the investigation
weaknesses: Minor lapses in expression and occasional repetition dilute precision
Methodology lacks primary research and data-collection procedures are only briefly described
Some justification for methodological choices (e.g., exclusion of factors) remains superficial
Redundancy in background sections slightly distracts from focus
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student excels in communicating the topic and formulating a sharply focused research question, maintaining strong connectivity throughout the essay. Although the methodology demonstrates informed source selection, it lacks depth in data collection and rationale. Minor expression issues and redundancy prevent a full-mark award, placing the performance at 5/6.

strengths: Source material is mostly integrated and supports key arguments on stakeholder reactions and financial impact
Subject knowledge is organized, coherent, and linked to real-world context
Business-management terminology is used correctly and consistently in most instances
Theory (e.g., CSR, stakeholder mapping) is effectively connected to the case study
weaknesses: Occasional inaccuracies and informal phrasing reduce precision
Some claims remain unsubstantiated or rely on opinion pieces
Financial analysis depth is uneven and largely qualitative
Terminology like âprocessâ conflates concepts without concrete data
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates clear and coherent knowledge, with mostly appropriate application of source material and accurate use of terminology. However, occasional mismatches, unsubstantiated claims, and uneven analytical depth limit the work to the mid-band, yielding 4/6.

strengths: Research is good in scope and relevance to the question, covering stakeholder, regulatory, and financial dimensions
Analysis is clear and draws logical links between external factors and strategic options
Arguments are structured and include critical evaluation of political and regulatory impacts
Limitations and methodological constraints are explicitly acknowledged
weaknesses: Quantitative and financial analysis remain shallow and sometimes based on assumptions
Evaluation of CSR vs. process improvement is occasionally one-sided
No primary data or triangulation weakens research depth
Some methodological assumptions (e.g., market-wide vs. company-specific effects) need refinement
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Critical thinking shows solid research quality, clear analysis, and a well-developed argument with partially critical evaluation. Despite strong relevance and structured reasoning, the depth of quantitative analysis and balance of evaluation is limited, positioning the work in the upper middle of the 0â12 band at 9.

strengths: Clearly appropriate structure with all required sections and guiding headings
Layout includes page numbers, word count, and formatted table of contents
In-text citations follow a recognizable style
weaknesses: Formatting glitches such as raw LaTeX code and inconsistent heading styles
Some sub-headings lack page numbers in the contents
Incomplete sentences and abrupt section endings disrupt flow
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The presentation meets the core requirements with a clear structure and mostly consistent formatting, earning a strong mid-band score. However, minor layout inconsistencies and formatting errors prevent full marks, resulting in a score of 3/4.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated in quotation marks, is sharply focused (single strategic choice for one company in a defined time frame) and remains the organising thread of the essay. Each analytical section repeatedly returns to deciding between CSR and process improvement, showing strong connectivity. The question is neither too broad nor ambiguous, and the scope (aftermath of March 2018 scandal) is consistently maintained. Some peripheral discussion (e.g., long CSR definition sections) slightly dilutes focus, so the top of the band is not fully justified.

A structured argument is developed that ultimately recommends enhancing the process element over CSR. Evaluation considers speed, cost, and stakeholder coverage, showing some critical thought. Yet the reasoning is occasionally one-sided (e.g., CSR framed largely as âdistractionâ) and lacks depth in weighing long-term impacts. The discussion is âwell-developed with partially critical evaluationâ, aligning with the mid-band.

The essay demonstrates clear understanding of CSR, marketing-mix theory, stakeholder mapping and STEEPLE analysis. Definitions are accurate and linked to real-world context. Knowledge is organised and coherent, yet depth is uneven: the financial analysis remains largely qualitative and some theoretical links (e.g., CSR as âdistractionâ) are simplistic. Sources are applied but not always critically interrogated, placing performance solidly in the 3-4 band.

Business-management terminology such as âstakeholder power-interestâ, âmarketing-mix processâ, âbrand imageâ, and âCSR categoriesâ is mostly used correctly and consistently. Occasional inaccuracies (e.g., categorising data protection solely under process) and informal phrasing prevent fully consistent precision, but overall usage is mostly accurate, meriting the mid-band score.

The student applies STEEPLE and stakeholder mapping to compare two strategic options and draws logical links between external factors and stakeholder needs. Nonetheless, quantitative analysis is shallow; financial comparisons are generic, and several conclusions rest on unverified assumptions about âimmediate resultsâ. This reflects clear but not advanced analysis, fitting the lower end of the 4-6 band.

Source material is mostly integrated (statistics on revenue loss, employee acceptance rates, market value effects) and used to support arguments about stakeholder reactions and financial impacts. Citations are frequent and generally appropriate, but some claims remain unsubstantiated or rely on opinion pieces. Occasional mismatches (e.g., conflating âprocessâ with privacy settings without concrete data) limit full relevance, so the strand fits the âmostly relevant and appropriateâ description.

The student relies on a wide variety of secondary sources (news articles, academic texts, documentary, corporate reports) and identifies analytical tools (STEEPLE, stakeholder map, basic financial comparison). Source selection is generally relevant to reputation, stakeholders and financial impact, showing some informed choice. However, the methodology lacks primary research, data-collection procedures are only briefly described, and limitations are acknowledged superficially. Consequently, evidence of *effective and informed* selection is partial rather than clear, placing the work mid-band.

The research base covers multiple stakeholder perspectives, regulatory changes and financial consequences that are clearly pertinent to the question, demonstrating good relevance. However, breadth outweighs depth; there is limited triangulation and no primary data, so the quality is âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ, warranting a mid-range score within the 4-6 band.

Page numbers, word count, and section headings are present; a table of contents is formatted, and in-text citations follow a recognizable style. There are some formatting glitches (LaTeX residuals, inconsistent bolding, image placeholder path), yet layout norms are largely respected. Hence the work meets the âlayout considerations present and correctly appliedâ descriptor.

The student articulates a precise, contextualised topic: evaluating whether Facebook should prioritise strengthening CSR or the process element of the marketing mix after the 2018 Cambridge Analytica scandal. Background information (company profile, nature of the scandal, stakeholder impacts) is presented logically and linked directly to the decision focus. Explanations of why the issue matters for Facebookâs recovery and stakeholder relations are clear and integrated throughout the introduction, demonstrating comprehensive communication. Minor lapses in expression and occasional repetition prevent a full-mark award, but overall the topic is conveyed accurately and effectively.

The essay follows required EE structure (title page, contents, introduction, methodology, analysis, conclusion, bibliography) and internal headings guide the reader. Minor inconsistencies (e.g., duplicated headings, abrupt page markers) detract slightly, but overall organisation is clearly appropriate, warranting the higher band.

annotations.comment: The final sentence of this section is cut off mid-phrase; complete or revise to maintain readability.
The research question is prominently and precisely stated in quotation marks, guiding the essayâs focus.
The aim statement succinctly links the question to stakeholder impact and recovery strategies.
The extensive CSR definition in the introduction could be condensed to maintain tighter alignment with the research question.
The title page clearly states the Extended Essay subject and level, meeting IB title requirements effectively.
The inclusion of the word count (3682) on the title page confirms compliance with the 4000-word limit.
The research question is repeated twice on the title page; consider removing duplication to streamline presentation.
Raw LaTeX (e.g. â\overlineâ) appears in the table of contents; reformat these entries for clarity.
A few sub-headings in the contents lack page numbers, which may confuse readers; ensure every entry is linked to a page.
Page numbers are consistently placed in the footer, aiding navigation through the essay.
The opening paragraph offers a coherent company background, effectively setting context.
Citation formatting varies (e.g. Downy vs. (Downy)), which affects consistency; standardize according to one style.
Methodology section critically acknowledges source bias (e.g., politically motivated documentary), demonstrating careful source selection.
The note excluding environmental factors in the STEEPLE requires more detailed rationale to justify its omission.
The âToolsâ heading uses a smaller font (h3) than âSourcesâ (h4), creating inconsistency in section styling.
The financial analysis limitation is explicitly acknowledged, reflecting strong critical thinking about methodological constraints.
The description of the stakeholder map tool succinctly explains its relevance to assessing stakeholder importance.
The CSR concept is defined accurately with scholarly sources, demonstrating effective use of terminology.
The section on marketing mix ends with an incomplete sentence (âcombine seven differentâ¦â); complete the thought for clarity.
The Cambridge Analytica background is repeated across sections; consider condensing to avoid redundancy.
Crisis management discussion integrates theory with multiple historical examples, strengthening the analysis depth.
The social factor in STEEPLE uses relevant survey statistics to support claims about consumer privacy concerns.
The brand image definition lacks a direct citation to Pahwa; include full reference to strengthen credibility.
The economic analysis conflates a market-wide stock crash with Facebook-specific impacts; refine to focus on company data.
The political factor section clearly links GDPR implementation to the scandal, demonstrating strong critical evaluation.
